title: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node
authors: William H. Sauer, Bruce A. Koplan

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node
items.title: Mechanism and Role of AV Node in Conduction Delay
items.content: The AV node is specialized for slow conduction due to its unique arrangement of gap junctions, connexins, extracellular matrix, fibroblasts, and lack of conductance in adjacent valvular tissue. This structure allows the AV node to act as an electrical 'gatekeeper' by creating a delay between atrial and ventricular activation.
keyPoints: The AV node has weak electrical coupling at its center to slow conduction.
Cells in the AV node have higher resting membrane potential, spontaneous depolarization during phase 4, and slower phase 0 depolarization mediated by calcium influx.
Normal automatic rate of the AV node is 20–60 beats/min.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node
items.title: Clinical Implications and Management of AV Conduction Block
items.content: Bradycardia due to compromised AV conduction can lead to fatigue, syncope, or death from asystole. Transient AV block is common in young adults due to high vagal tone but persistent failure is rare in healthy adults. Persistent AV block is more common with myocardial ischemia, aging, fibrosis, or cardiac infiltrative diseases. Permanent pacing is the reliable therapy for symptomatic bradycardia from AV conduction block.
keyPoints: Transient AV conduction block can occur in up to 10% of young adults due to high vagal tone.
Incidence of acquired and persistent AV conduction failure in healthy adults is estimated at 1 per 5000 per year.
Permanent pacing is indicated for symptomatic bradycardia from AV conduction block, especially if infra-Hisian.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node

items.subtopic: STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE
items.title: Anatomical Landmarks and Pathways of the AV Node
items.content: The AV node is a small, critical structure located beneath the right atrial endocardium at the apex of the triangle of Koch. It has distinct regions including the transition cell zone, compact AV node, and penetrating part of the His bundle. The AV node facilitates conduction from the SA node via intra-atrial pathways with higher velocity.
keyPoints: The AV node is approximately 1 × 3 × 5 mm in size.
Located at the apex of the triangle of Koch, defined by the coronary sinus ostium, septal tricuspid valve annulus, and tendon of Todaro.
Rightward inferior extension spreads along the tricuspid valve toward the coronary sinus; leftward extension spreads from the compact node along the tendon of Todaro.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE

items.subtopic: STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE
items.title: Electrophysiological Properties and Ionic Currents in AV Node Cells
items.content: AV nodal cells exhibit unique electrophysiological properties due to their distinct ionic current profiles. These include a resting membrane potential of approximately -60 mV, low amplitude action potentials with slow upstrokes, and spontaneous phase 4 depolarization.
keyPoints: Resting membrane potential is ~-60 mV.
Phase 0 upstroke velocity <10 V/s due to L-type calcium current (I_{Ca-L}).
Phase 4 depolarization involves funny current (I_L), I_{Ca-L}, T-type calcium current, and sodium-calcium exchanger current.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE

items.subtopic: STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE
items.title: Autonomic Innervation and Blood Supply of the AV Node
items.content: The AV node receives autonomic innervation from both sympathetic and parasympathetic nerves, which modulate conduction. Its blood supply primarily comes from the AV nodal artery, with dual sources depending on coronary dominance.
keyPoints: AV node is highly innervated by postganglionic sympathetic and parasympathetic nerves.
Blood supply: 80-90% from right coronary artery (AV nodal artery), 10% from left circumflex.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE

items.subtopic: ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)
items.title: Distinguishing First-Degree from Second-Degree AV Block
items.content: First-degree AV block is characterized by a fixed prolongation of the PR interval (>200 ms) and typically involves delay within the AV node. In contrast, second-degree AV block involves intermittent failure of conduction with two subtypes: Mobitz I (Wenckebach), which shows progressive PR interval prolongation before dropped QRS complexes, and Mobitz II, characterized by a fixed PR interval before dropped beats.
keyPoints: First-degree AV block: PR interval >200 ms
Mobitz I (Type I) second-degree AV block: Progressive PR interval prolongation before dropped QRS
Mobitz II (Type II) second-degree AV block: Fixed PR interval before dropped beat, more serious implications including risk of sudden death
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)

items.subtopic: ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)
items.title: Identifying Infranodal Block and Implications for Pacing
items.content: Type II second-degree AV block is infranodal, associated with a fixed PR interval before dropped beats, and requires permanent pacing due to unreliable escape rhythm. Distinguishing between type I and II in 2:1 block involves assessing PR interval (<160 ms) and QRS width (wider than normal suggests type II).
keyPoints: Type II second-degree AV block: Infranodal, fixed PR interval before dropped beat, requires permanent pacing
PR interval <160 ms and wide QRS suggest type II block in 2:1 AV block
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA
items.title: Iatrogenic Causes of AV Block
items.content: AV block can result from various iatrogenic causes, particularly related to cardiac interventions. Cardiac surgeries such as valve replacements and procedures like percutaneous transcatheter aortic valve replacement carry risks for AV block. Additionally, catheter ablation for atrial arrhythmias has a small risk of causing AV block.
keyPoints: Cardiac surgery, especially involving the aortic or tricuspid valves, can damage the AV conduction system.
Percutaneous transcatheter aortic valve replacement and septal myectomy/ablation carry risks for AV block.
<1% risk of AV block with catheter ablation for atrial arrhythmias.
Medications like beta blockers, verapamil, diltiazem, and digoxin can cause iatrogenic AV block.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA
items.title: AV Block in Myocardial Ischemia
items.content: Myocardial ischemia, particularly from coronary artery disease, can lead to transient or persistent AV block. Inferior MI often results in first- or second-degree AV block due to right coronary artery involvement, while anterior MI is more likely to cause higher-grade blocks and has a worse prognosis.
keyPoints: Coronary spasm may produce transient AV block, especially with right coronary artery ischemia.
10–25% of acute myocardial infarction patients develop transient AV block.
Inferior MI often results in first- or second-degree AV block; anterior MI can lead to higher-grade blocks and worse prognosis.
High-grade AV block in inferior MI is proximal to the His bundle in 90% of cases, often reversible with pharmacologic therapy.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA
items.title: Management and Prognosis of AV Block in MI
items.content: The management of AV block during myocardial infarction depends on the location of the infarct. Inferior MIs with high-grade AV block often do not require temporary pacing due to reversibility, while anterior MIs necessitate pacing due to extensive damage and higher mortality.
keyPoints: Inferior MI-related AV block is often reversible; narrow junctional escape rhythms occur at >40 beats/min without needing a pacemaker.
Anterior MI-related high-grade AV block indicates extensive infarction, requiring temporary pacing.
CHB in anterior MI may follow RBB block due to arterial supply patterns.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA

items.subtopic: INFECTIOUS CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | AUTONOMIC AND FUNCTIONAL CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | ACQUIRED AV BLOCK FROM FIBROSIS AND INFILTRATIVE CARDIOMYOPATHIES
items.title: Management of Lyme Carditis-Induced AV Block
items.content: Lyme carditis, caused by Borrelia burgdorferi infection, often results in AV block at the level of the AV node. The condition typically presents with a narrow junctional escape rhythm greater than 40 beats per minute and usually resolves within one week following antibiotic therapy.
keyPoints: Lyme carditis is a common infectious cause of AV block.
AV block occurs at the AV node level, presenting as a narrow junctional escape rhythm >40 bpm.
Resolution typically occurs within 1 week with appropriate antibiotic treatment.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · INFECTIOUS CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | AUTONOMIC AND FUNCTIONAL CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | ACQUIRED AV BLOCK FROM FIBROSIS AND INFILTRATIVE CARDIOMYOPATHIES

items.subtopic: INFECTIOUS CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | AUTONOMIC AND FUNCTIONAL CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | ACQUIRED AV BLOCK FROM FIBROSIS AND INFILTRATIVE CARDIOMYOPATHIES
items.title: AV Block in Infiltrative Cardiomyopathies and Fibrosis
items.content: Idiopathic progressive fibrosis of the conduction system is a common cause of AV block, often beginning in the fourth decade of life. This process can be accelerated by conditions such as atherosclerosis, hypertension, and diabetes mellitus.
keyPoints: Progressive fibrosis of the conduction system commonly causes AV block.
The degenerative changes typically start in the fourth decade of life.
Aging-related sclerosis affects the left cardiac skeleton, including the ventricular septum and central fibrous body.
Risk factors accelerating this process include atherosclerosis, hypertension, and diabetes mellitus.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · INFECTIOUS CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | AUTONOMIC AND FUNCTIONAL CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | ACQUIRED AV BLOCK FROM FIBROSIS AND INFILTRATIVE CARDIOMYOPATHIES

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)
items.title: When to Use Advanced Imaging for Younger Patients with AV Block
items.content: Advanced imaging such as chest CT, cardiac MRI, or PET scan is recommended for patients under 60 years old presenting with AV node block to evaluate for infiltrative heart diseases like sarcoidosis. This recommendation arises from the fact that age-dependent progressive fibrosis of the conduction system is a common cause.
keyPoints: Age ≤ 60 years warrants advanced imaging for AV block
Evaluate for infiltrative heart disease such as sarcoidosis
Advanced imaging includes chest CT, cardiac MRI, or PET scan
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)
items.title: Distinguishing Between AV Node and Infranodal Conduction Blocks Using Pharmacologic Stress
items.content: Vagal maneuvers, carotid sinus massage, atropine, isoproterenol, and exercise can help differentiate between AV node and infranodal conduction blocks. Vagal stimulation slows AV nodal conduction but has less effect on infranodal tissue, while atropine and isoproterenol enhance AV nodal conduction.
keyPoints: Vagal maneuvers slow AV nodal conduction
Atropine and isoproterenol improve AV nodal conduction
Exercise increases heart rate in congenital CHB with narrow QRS
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)
items.title: Interpreting His Bundle Electrogram for Conduction Block Localization
items.content: His bundle electrograms provide precise information on the location of AV conduction block. The AH interval (<130 ms) represents AV nodal conduction, while the HV interval (≤55 ms) indicates His-Purkinje system conduction.
keyPoints: AH interval < 130 ms is normal for AV nodal conduction
HV interval ≤ 55 ms is normal for His-Purkinje system conduction
His bundle electrogram assesses intra-atrial, AV nodal, and infranodal conduction times
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block
items.title: HV Interval and Indications for Pacing in Bundle Branch Block
items.content: In patients with bundle branch block, a prolonged HV interval indicates an increased risk of progression to higher-grade AV conduction block. An HV interval greater than 100 ms is associated with a significant annual incidence (approximately 10%) of complete AV block, thus indicating the need for pacing.
keyPoints: Prolonged HV interval (>100 ms) in bundle branch block patients indicates increased risk of higher-grade AV block.
Annual incidence of complete AV block approaches 10% with an HV interval >100 ms.
Indication for pacemaker implantation is almost always warranted if there's acquired CHB, even intermittently.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block
items.title: Criteria and Techniques for Temporary Pacing in AV Block
items.content: Temporary pacing is indicated based on patient symptoms, hemodynamic status, and level of AV block. A wide-complex escape rhythm at a rate lower than 45 beats/min generally warrants urgent temporary pacing due to its unreliability. Transcutaneous pacing is effective but limited by discomfort; transvenous pacing offers more reliable long-term management.
keyPoints: Temporary pacing indicated by symptoms, hemodynamic status, and level of AV block.
Wide-complex escape rhythm at 30 beats/min warrants urgent temporary pacing.
Transcutaneous pacing is effective but limited by discomfort; transvenous pacing is more reliable.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block

items.subtopic: PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION
items.title: Indications for Permanent Pacemaker Implantation in AV Conduction Block
items.content: Permanent pacing is recommended for patients with acquired Mobitz type II AV block, high-grade AV block, or third-degree AV block that are not reversible or physiologic. In other types of AV block without progressive conduction abnormalities, pacing should be considered only if symptoms correlate with the block.
keyPoints: Permanent pacing is recommended for acquired Mobitz type II AV block, high-grade AV block, and irreversible third-degree AV block regardless of symptoms.
In non-reversible or physiologic cases, permanent pacing is indicated irrespective of symptom presence.
For other AV blocks without progressive abnormalities, consider pacing only if symptoms correlate with the block.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION
items.title: Pacing in Asymptomatic and Special Circumstances of AV Block
items.content: In asymptomatic patients, pacemaker implantation should be considered for acquired complete heart block (CHB) with cardiac enlargement or left ventricular dysfunction, waking heart rates ≤ 40 beats/min, and second-degree AV block with intra- or infra-His location or wide QRS complex. Special circumstances include profound first-degree AV block with left ventricular dysfunction where a shorter AV interval improves hemodynamics.
keyPoints: Consider pacing in asymptomatic patients with acquired CHB, cardiac enlargement, left ventricular dysfunction, and waking heart rates ≤ 40 beats/min.
Pacing may be indicated for second-degree AV block if intra- or infra-His or associated with a wide QRS complex.
In special cases, profound first-degree AV block with hemodynamic improvement from a shorter AV interval warrants pacing.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM
items.title: Importance of Physiologic Ventricular Pacing in Heart Failure Prevention
items.content: In patients with left ventricular ejection fraction <50% and AV block requiring permanent pacing, physiologic ventricular pacing is preferred over right ventricular pacing to prevent heart failure. Techniques such as Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy (CRT) or His bundle pacing are used to achieve more physiologic cardiac contraction.
keyPoints: Physiologic ventricular pacing is preferred for patients with left ventricular ejection fraction <50% and AV block requiring >40% ventricular pacing.
Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) involves placing an additional lead in the coronary sinus to allow simultaneous right and lateral left ventricle pacing.
His bundle pacing recruits the specialized conduction system for a more physiologic cardiac contraction.
Physiologic pacing can improve outcomes and mortality in appropriately selected patients.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM
items.title: Advantages of Leadless Pacemakers in Specific Patient Populations
items.content: Leadless pacemakers, implanted via the femoral vein into the right atrium or ventricle, offer advantages such as lower long-term infection rates and no risk of lead fracture. They are particularly useful for patients with vascular access limitations.
keyPoints: Leadless pacemakers are self-contained devices implanted directly into the heart chambers.
They can provide single- or dual-chamber pacing while preserving AV synchrony.
Long-term infection rates are lower due to the absence of intravascular wires or subcutaneous generators.
No risk of lead fracture, making them suitable for patients with vascular access limitations.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node
items.question: What is the primary function of the AV node in cardiac conduction?
options: To generate action potentials for ventricular contraction.
To serve as an electrical 'gatekeeper' to the ventricle by slowing conduction.
To maintain a high intrinsic rate of 60–100 beats/min.
To provide fast sodium channels for rapid depolarization.
items.explanation: The AV node slows conduction and serves as an electrical 'gatekeeper' to the ventricle.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node
items.question: What is the normal automatic rate range for the AV node?
items.explanation: The normal automatic rate of the AV node is 20–60 beats/min, which can serve as a backup heart rate when the SA node fails.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node
items.question: Which condition is most likely to cause transient AV conduction block in young adults?
items.explanation: Transient AV conduction block in young adults is most likely due to high vagal tone, found in up to 10% of this population.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node

items.subtopic: STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE
items.question: Which region of the AV node is primarily responsible for phase 0 depolarization?
options: The penetrating part of the His bundle
items.explanation: Phase 0 depolarization in the compact AV node is primarily due to L-type calcium current (I_Ca-L).
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE

items.subtopic: STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE
items.question: What characterizes the decremental conduction observed in atrionodal transitional connections?
options: Conduction velocity increases with rapid rates of stimulation.
Conduction velocity remains constant regardless of stimulation rate.
Conduction velocity decreases with increasingly rapid rates of stimulation.
Conduction is unaffected by autonomic input.
items.explanation: Atrionodal transitional connections exhibit decremental conduction, defined as slowing of conduction with increasingly rapid rates of stimulation.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE

items.subtopic: STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE
items.question: Which cells in the AV node complex have a resting membrane potential similar to sinus node myocytes?
items.explanation: Myocytes that constitute the compact AV node have a resting membrane potential of ~-60 mV, similar to sinus node myocytes.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE

items.subtopic: STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE
items.question: What is responsible for the rapid upstroke (phase 0) in Purkinje cells?
items.explanation: The rapid upstroke in Purkinje cells is due to the fast sodium current (I_Na1).
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE

items.subtopic: ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)
items.question: Which of the following best describes a characteristic feature of first-degree AV block?
options: Fixed prolongation of the PR interval (>200 ms)
Intermittent failure of conduction between the atrium and ventricle
Progressive prolongation of the PR interval before dropped QRS complexes
Failed AV conduction preceded by a fixed PR interval
items.explanation: First-degree AV block is characterized by a fixed prolongation of the PR interval (>200 ms).
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)

items.subtopic: ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)
items.question: In which type of second-degree AV block does progressive prolongation of the PR interval occur before a dropped QRS complex?
options: Type I (Mobitz I, Wenckebach) AV block
items.explanation: Type I (Mobitz I, Wenckebach) AV block involves progressive prolongation of the PR interval before a dropped QRS complex.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)

items.subtopic: ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)
items.question: Which type of second-degree AV block is associated with a fixed PR interval and requires permanent pacing?
options: Type I (Mobitz I, Wenckebach) AV block
items.explanation: Type II second-degree AV block is associated with a fixed PR interval and requires permanent pacing due to its serious implications.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)

items.question: Which type of myocardial infarction is more commonly associated with high-grade atrioventricular (AV) block?
items.explanation: High-grade AV block is more commonly associated with anterior myocardial infarction due to extensive infarction and distal block.

options: At the level of the bundle branches
items.explanation: High-grade AV block in inferior myocardial infarction is typically located proximal to the His bundle.

items.question: What type of escape rhythm is typically associated with high-grade AV block in inferior myocardial infarction?
items.explanation: High-grade AV block in inferior myocardial infarction is associated with narrow junctional escape rhythms.

items.question: Which imaging modality may be used to evaluate for infiltrative heart disease such as sarcoidosis in patients with AV node block developing at a younger age?
items.explanation: Advanced imaging such as cardiac MRI is indicated for evaluating infiltrative heart disease in younger patients with AV node block.

items.question: What diagnostic maneuver may be used to assess the effect of vagal stimulation on conduction through the AV node?
items.explanation: Vagal maneuvers such as carotid sinus massage slow conduction in the AV node and are used to assess its effect.

items.question: What does a properly recorded His bundle electrogram reveal about intra-atrial, AV nodal, and infranodal conduction times?
options: It reveals only the time from atrial to ventricular activation.
It provides information on local atrial activity, the His electrogram, and local ventricular activation.
It shows only the HV interval.
items.explanation: A properly recorded His bundle electrogram reveals local atrial activity, the His electrogram, and local ventricular activation.

items.explanation: The AH interval, representing conduction through the AV node, is normally <130 ms.

items.question: In patients with an HV interval >100 ms and asymptomatic bundle branch block, what is the annual incidence of complete AV block?
items.explanation: An HV interval >100 ms in patients with asymptomatic bundle branch block is associated with an annual incidence of complete AV block approaching 10%.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block
items.question: What is the recommended management for acquired complete heart block (CHB), even if intermittent?
items.explanation: In patients with acquired complete heart block, even if intermittent, pacemaker implantation is almost always indicated.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block
items.question: Which pacing technique is most expeditious for acute management when pacing is indicated?
items.explanation: The most expeditious technique for acute management when pacing is indicated is transcutaneous pacing.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block

items.subtopic: PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION
items.question: In which patients is permanent pacing recommended regardless of symptoms?
options: Patients with acquired Mobitz type I AV block and mild first-degree AV block.
Patients with high-grade AV block or third-degree AV block that is not reversible.
Patients with transient second- or third-degree AV block without bundle branch block.
items.explanation: Permanent pacing is recommended in patients with high-grade AV block or third-degree AV block that is not reversible, regardless of symptoms.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION
items.question: When should pacemaker implantation be considered for asymptomatic second-degree AV block?
options: If the block is located at or below the His bundle.
In all cases of asymptomatic second-degree AV block.
Only if associated with a narrow QRS complex.
If there is no history of neuromuscular disease.
items.explanation: Pacemaker implantation should be considered for asymptomatic second-degree AV block if the block is intra- or infra-His or associated with a wide QRS complex.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION
items.question: In which situation is pacing generally not indicated during acute myocardial infarction (MI)?
options: Persistent second- or third-degree AV block.
Transient second- or third-degree AV block with bundle branch block.
Transient AV block without intraventricular conduction delays.
First-degree AV block developing in the setting of preexisting bundle branch block.
items.explanation: Pacing is generally not indicated for transient AV block during acute MI in the absence of intraventricular conduction delays.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM
items.question: Which pacing technique is preferred in patients with left ventricular ejection fraction <50% and AV block to prevent heart failure?
items.explanation: Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) is preferred to provide more physiologic ventricular activation and prevent heart failure.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM
items.question: What advantage do leadless pacemakers have over traditional pacemakers?
options: Higher infection rate due to intravascular wires
Lower long-term infection rate and no risk of lead fracture
items.explanation: Leadless pacemakers have a lower long-term infection rate and no risk of lead fracture.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM
items.question: In patients with sinus rhythm and AV block, why might dual-chamber pacing be justified despite its modest increase in risk?
options: To avoid complications associated with single-chamber pacing
Because it is less expensive than single-chamber pacing
Dual-chamber pacing has no risks compared to single-chamber pacing
items.explanation: The modest increase in risk with dual-chamber pacemaker implantation is justified to avoid complications of single-chamber pacing.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node
items.statement: Persistent AV conduction block is common in healthy adult populations.
items.explanation: Acquired and persistent failure of AV conduction is rare in healthy adults, with an estimated incidence of 1 per 5000 per year.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node
items.statement: Permanent pacing may be indicated for infra-Hisian block even without symptoms.
items.explanation: Pacemakers may be indicated in some forms of AV block, such as infra-Hisian block, due to the risk of life-threatening asystole.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node

items.subtopic: STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE
items.statement: The AV node has a robust inward rectifier potassium current (I_K1).
items.explanation: AV nodal cells lack a robust inward rectifier potassium current (I_K1).
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE

items.subtopic: STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE
items.statement: The bundle branches are insulated from ventricular myocardium.
items.explanation: The His bundle and the bundle branches are indeed insulated from ventricular myocardium.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE

items.subtopic: ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)
items.statement: Mobitz I and IV block typically involve the His bundle or Purkinje system.
items.explanation: Mobitz I and IV blocks typically involve the AV node, not the His bundle or Purkinje system.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)

items.subtopic: ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)
items.statement: In complete heart block, there is complete AV dissociation with a ventricular rate slower than the atrial rate.
items.explanation: Complete heart block involves complete AV dissociation and a slower ventricular rate compared to the atrial rate.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC DEFINITIONS OF AV CONDUCTION BLOCK (TABLE 252-1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA
items.statement: Calcification of the mitral valve annulus is a common cause of AV block.
items.explanation: Mitral calcification is less commonly a cause of AV block compared to aortic valve calcification.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA
items.statement: Temporary pacing is typically required for high-grade AV block in inferior myocardial infarction.
items.explanation: High-grade AV block in inferior MI often has narrow junctional escape rhythms and may not require temporary pacing.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA

items.subtopic: INFECTIOUS CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | AUTONOMIC AND FUNCTIONAL CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | ACQUIRED AV BLOCK FROM FIBROSIS AND INFILTRATIVE CARDIOMYOPATHIES
items.statement: AV block due to Lyme carditis typically resolves within 1 week of antibiotic therapy.
items.explanation: AV block in Lyme carditis usually improves with antibiotics, although it can take longer in some cases.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · INFECTIOUS CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | AUTONOMIC AND FUNCTIONAL CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | ACQUIRED AV BLOCK FROM FIBROSIS AND INFILTRATIVE CARDIOMYOPATHIES

items.subtopic: INFECTIOUS CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | AUTONOMIC AND FUNCTIONAL CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | ACQUIRED AV BLOCK FROM FIBROSIS AND INFILTRATIVE CARDIOMYOPATHIES
items.statement: Idiopathic progressive fibrosis of the conduction system is reversible.
items.explanation: Fibrosis in the conduction system typically leads to permanent changes.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · INFECTIOUS CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | AUTONOMIC AND FUNCTIONAL CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | ACQUIRED AV BLOCK FROM FIBROSIS AND INFILTRATIVE CARDIOMYOPATHIES

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)
items.statement: Mobitz I second-degree AV block at atrial cycle lengths >500 ms in the absence of high vagal tone is considered a normal response.
items.explanation: Mobitz I second-degree AV block at atrial cycle lengths >500 ms without high vagal tone is abnormal.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)
items.statement: Type II second-degree AV block typically occurs in the AV node.
items.explanation: Type II second-degree AV block is typically infranodal, often in the His-Purkinje system.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block
items.statement: Electrophysiologic testing has a significant role in the management of acquired complete heart block.
items.explanation: In patients with acquired CHB, even if intermittent, there is little role for electrophysiologic testing.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block
items.statement: A narrow-complex junctional escape rhythm at 45 beats/min with no symptoms requires urgent temporary pacing.
items.explanation: A narrow-complex junctional escape of 45 beats/min with no symptoms does not warrant urgent temporary pacing.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block

items.subtopic: PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION
items.statement: Permanent pacing is recommended for all patients with first-degree AV block.
items.explanation: Permanent pacing is not generally recommended for first-degree AV block unless it is marked and associated with neuromuscular diseases affecting the conduction system.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION
items.statement: Pacing may be indicated in asymptomatic patients with profound first-degree AV block and left ventricular dysfunction if a shorter AV interval improves hemodynamics.
items.explanation: In cases of profound first-degree AV block with left ventricular dysfunction, pacing can improve hemodynamics by shortening the AV interval.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM
items.statement: His bundle pacing leads to a less physiologic cardiac contraction.
items.explanation: His bundle pacing recruits the specialized conduction system, leading to a more physiologic cardiac contraction.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM
items.statement: Leadless pacemakers can be implanted via the femoral vein into the right atrium and ventricle.
items.explanation: Leadless pacemakers are implanted via the femoral vein into the right atrium and ventricle.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · SELECTION OF PACING MODE AND SYSTEM

items.subtopic: The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node
items.assertion: AV node dysfunction can lead to bradycardia.
items.reason: Compromised conduction across the AV node results in slow ventricular rates.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why AV node dysfunction leads to bradycardia.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · The Bradyarrhythmias: Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node

items.subtopic: STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE
items.assertion: The compact AV node has high-input resistance.
items.reason: This is due to the sparse expression of gap junction channels and increased extracellular volume.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE

items.subtopic: STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE
items.assertion: The AV node is innervated by both sympathetic and parasympathetic autonomic input.
items.reason: This innervation can either slow or enhance conduction through the AV node.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · STRUCTURE AND PHYSIOLOGY OF THE AV NODE

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA
items.assertion: High-grade AV block in anterior myocardial infarction is associated with increased mortality.
items.reason: Anterior MI often involves extensive infarction and distal block to the AV node, leading to worse outcomes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA
items.assertion: AV block is a common complication of surgical repair for ventricular septal defects.
items.reason: The conduction system is often damaged during the surgery for ventricular septal defect repairs.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as AV block is a rare complication, but the reason could be true in general surgical contexts.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · ETIOLOGY OF AV CONDUCTION DISEASE | IATROGENIC CAUSES | AV BLOCK IN THE SETTING OF MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA

items.subtopic: INFECTIOUS CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | AUTONOMIC AND FUNCTIONAL CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | ACQUIRED AV BLOCK FROM FIBROSIS AND INFILTRATIVE CARDIOMYOPATHIES
items.assertion: AV block can be caused by heightened vagal tone during sleep.
items.reason: Heightened vagal tone is associated with reversible functional causes of AV block.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · INFECTIOUS CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | AUTONOMIC AND FUNCTIONAL CAUSES OF AV BLOCK | ACQUIRED AV BLOCK FROM FIBROSIS AND INFILTRATIVE CARDIOMYOPATHIES

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)
items.assertion: Vagal maneuvers and carotid sinus massage slow conduction through the AV node.
items.reason: These maneuvers have less effect on infranodal tissue and may improve conduction due to reduced activation of distal tissues.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why vagal maneuvers affect AV nodal conduction.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)
items.assertion: Atropine can be used to reverse high vagal tone in patients with prolonged AH interval.
items.reason: If AH prolongation persists after atropine administration, intrinsic AV node disease is likely.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; atropine reverses high vagal tone, and persistent AH prolongation suggests intrinsic AV node disease.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · CONGENITAL AV BLOCK | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block
items.assertion: Reversible causes should be eliminated in the management of AV block.
items.reason: Elimination of unnecessary medications and correction of electrolyte abnormalities can increase heart rate.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: The Bradyarrhythmias Disorders of the Atrioventricular Node · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | Acute Management of AV Conduction Block
